agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress channels (mobile, partner, internal), a shared policy engine for auth and rate limiting, and a service mesh for internal traffic isolation. This eliminates auth inconsistency, centralizes cross-cutting concerns, and creates clear security boundaries between external and internal traffic.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Edge Gateway (Mobile Ingress)","responsibility":"Terminates TLS, authenticates mobile users via JWT/OAuth2, enforces 100 req/min per-user rate limits, routes to backend services. Handles token refresh flows and device fingerprinting.","boundaries":"Only accepts traffic from public internet on mobile endpoints. Cannot reach internal-only services. Strips all internal headers before forwarding."},{"name":"Edge Gateway (Partner Ingress)","responsibility":"Terminates TLS, validates API keys (with optional mTLS for premium partners), enforces 1,000 req/min per-partner rate limits, manages webhook registration and delivery. Provides partner-specific request transformation and versioned API routing.","boundaries":"Only accepts traffic from registered partner IP allowlists or via mTLS. Isolated from mobile ingress at the network level. Cannot reach internal-only services."},{"name":"Internal Gateway","responsibility":"Routes internal service-to-service traffic. No authentication but enforces network-level isolation (only reachable from private subnet/service mesh). Provides service discovery, circuit breaking, and observability injection.","boundaries":"Unreachable from any external network. Deployed in a private subnet with no public IP. Only services within the mesh can route through it."},{"name":"Policy Engine","responsibility":"Shared library/sidecar that encapsulates all auth strategies (JWT validation, API key lookup, mTLS verification) and rate limiting logic (token bucket per identity). Consumed by both edge gateways as a single source of truth for access policy.","boundaries":"Stateless decision engine. Reads policy config from a central policy store. Rate limit counters stored in Redis (shared state). Does not hold business logic."},{"name":"Rate Limit Store (Redis)","responsibility":"Stores sliding-window or token-bucket counters keyed by user ID (mobile) or partner ID (B2B). Provides atomic increment operations for distributed rate limiting across gateway replicas.","boundaries":"Accessible only from gateway pods. Not exposed externally. Data is ephemeral — TTLs match rate limit windows."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages partner webhook subscriptions, serializes domain events into webhook payloads, delivers with exponential backoff retry, and logs delivery status. Partners configure endpoints via the Partner Ingress API.","boundaries":"Consumes domain events from an internal message bus. Outbound-only to partner endpoints. Stores subscription config in its own database."},{"name":"Backend Services","responsibility":"Core logistics domain services (orders, tracking, inventory, routing). Stripped of all auth and rate limiting logic — they trust the gateway layer and focus purely on business logic.","boundaries":"Only reachable via gateway layer (edge or internal). Each service owns its own data store. Communicates with other services via internal gateway or async events."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"Edge Gateway (Mobile Ingress)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT in Authorization header. OpenAPI-defined REST endpoints. Gateway validates JWT signature and claims before forwarding.","coupling_type":"loose — consumers depend on published OpenAPI contract, not service internals"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"Edge Gateway (Partner Ingress)","contract":"HTTPS with X-API-Key header (or mTLS client cert). Versioned REST API (URL path versioning: /v1/, /v2/). Gateway validates key against partner registry.","coupling_type":"loose — versioned API contract with backward compatibility guarantees"},{"from":"Edge Gateway (Mobile Ingress)","to":"Policy Engine","contract":"Synchronous call (in-process library or local sidecar). Input: request context (token, IP, path). Output: allow/deny decision + identity claims.","coupling_type":"tight — gateway depends on policy engine for every request, but engine is stateless and horizontally scalable"},{"from":"Edge Gateway (Partner Ingress)","to":"Policy Engine","contract":"Same interface as mobile ingress. Input includes API key and partner context. Output includes partner tier for rate limit selection.","coupling_type":"tight — same coupling profile as mobile ingress"},{"from":"Policy Engine","to":"Rate Limit Store (Redis)","contract":"Atomic INCR with TTL on composite key (identity_type:identity_id:window). Returns current count. Gateway compares against tier limit.","coupling_type":"operational — Redis is a runtime dependency; if unavailable, gateway must fail-open or fail-closed per policy"},{"from":"Edge Gateways","to":"Backend Services","contract":"Internal HTTP/gRPC with X-Authenticated-Identity and X-Consumer-Type headers injected by gateway. Services trust these headers unconditionally.","coupling_type":"moderate — services depend on gateway-injected headers for identity context"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Gateway","contract":"Service mesh routing (e.g., Kubernetes service DNS or Envoy). No auth headers. Caller identity established by mTLS within the mesh.","coupling_type":"infrastructure — coupled to service mesh but decoupled from business auth"},{"from":"Backend Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message bus (e.g., 'order.shipped', 'delivery.completed'). Dispatcher subscribes and translates to partner webhook payloads.","coupling_type":"loose — async event-driven, services don't know about webhooks"}],"data_flow":"External request → Edge Gateway (TLS termination) → Policy Engine (auth + rate limit check against Redis) → if allowed, forward to Backend Service with identity headers injected → Backend Service processes request → response flows back through gateway → gateway adds standard headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset). For webhooks: Backend Service emits domain event → Message Bus → Webhook Dispatcher → HTTP POST to partner endpoint with HMAC signature. For internal: Service A → Internal Gateway (service mesh) → Service B, no auth overhead, network-isolated.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Backend for Frontend (BFF)","rationale":"Separate ingress points per consumer type allows optimized auth strategies, rate limits, and API shapes without a one-size-fits-all gateway that becomes a god object."},{"pattern":"Token Bucket Rate Limiting with Redis","rationale":"Distributed rate limiting across multiple gateway replicas requires shared state. Redis provides atomic operations with sub-millisecond latency. Token bucket allows burst tolerance while enforcing average rate."},{"pattern":"Policy Engine (externalized authorization)","rationale":"Centralizing auth and rate limit decisions in a shared component eliminates the current inconsistency across services. Single place to audit, update, and test access policy."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Services currently handle auth independently. The gateway should be introduced incrementally — route one service at a time through the gateway, strip its auth code, verify, repeat. Avoids big-bang migration risk."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Webhook Delivery","rationale":"Decouples webhook concerns from core business logic. Services emit domain events; the webhook dispatcher handles serialization, delivery, retries, and failure tracking independently."},{"pattern":"Service Mesh for Internal Traffic","rationale":"Network-level isolation of internal traffic without application-level auth overhead. mTLS between services provides identity without token management."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Access Control","classification":"generic","aggregates":["UserSession (mobile JWT lifecycle)","PartnerCredential (API key, mTLS cert, IP allowlist)","RateLimitPolicy (tier definitions, limit configurations)"]},{"name":"API Routing","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["RouteDefinition (path → service mapping, version routing)","TransformationRule (request/response rewriting per consumer type)"]},{"name":"Webhook Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription (partner, event types, endpoint URL, HMAC secret)","DeliveryAttempt (timestamp, status code, retry count, next retry)"]},{"name":"Core Logistics","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Tracking","Inventory"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Access Control","downstream":"API Routing","relationship":"Conformist — routing layer conforms to auth decisions without translation"},{"upstream":"Core Logistics","downstream":"Webhook Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — logistics publishes domain events, webhook management subscribes and transforms for partner delivery"},{"upstream":"Access Control","downstream":"Core Logistics","relationship":"Open Host Service — gateway exposes identity via standardized headers; logistics services consume without coupling to auth implementation"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single logical gateway with separate ingress points per consumer type (BFF-style) vs single unified gateway","gains":"Each ingress can evolve independently. Mobile can adopt GraphQL later without affecting partners. Rate limit tiers are cleanly separated. Security blast radius is contained per channel.","sacrifices":"More infrastructure to manage (3 gateway deployments vs 1). Some policy logic duplication unless shared via library/sidecar.","alternatives_considered":["Single unified gateway with path-based consumer routing — simpler infra but becomes a coupling point and config complexity grows","Full service mesh with no gateway — too much auth logic pushed to each service, which is the current problem"]},{"decision":"Redis for distributed rate limiting vs in-memory per-instance rate limiting","gains":"Accurate global rate limits across all gateway replicas. No split-brain where user hits different instances and bypasses limits.","sacrifices":"Redis becomes a runtime dependency. Adds ~1ms latency per request. Requires Redis HA setup.","alternatives_considered":["In-memory rate limiting per instance — simpler but inaccurate with multiple replicas","Database-backed counters — too slow for per-request checks"]},{"decision":"Fail-open vs fail-closed when Redis is unavailable","gains":"Fail-open preserves availability during Redis outages at the cost of temporarily unenforced rate limits.","sacrifices":"Brief window where rate limits are not enforced. Requires monitoring to detect and alert.","alternatives_considered":["Fail-closed — safer but causes total outage if Redis goes down, which is worse for a logistics platform where availability matters more than perfect rate limiting"]},{"decision":"Strip auth from backend services entirely vs defense-in-depth","gains":"Simpler backend services, single responsibility, no auth code duplication.","sacrifices":"If gateway is bypassed (misconfiguration, network policy error), services are unprotected. Requires strict network policy enforcement.","alternatives_considered":["Defense-in-depth with lightweight auth in services too — safer but perpetuates the inconsistency problem and adds latency. Recommend network policy enforcement + periodic penetration testing instead."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Auth strategy per consumer type","evolution_path":"Each ingress gateway independently evolves its auth. Mobile can move from JWT to passkeys. Partners can adopt OAuth2 client credentials. No cross-impact."},{"area":"API protocol per consumer","evolution_path":"Mobile ingress can adopt GraphQL or BFF aggregation patterns. Partner ingress stays REST. Internal can move to gRPC. Gateway layer absorbs protocol translation."},{"area":"Rate limit tiers","evolution_path":"Policy engine config can define arbitrary tiers (free partner, premium partner, enterprise). Adding a tier is a config change, not a code change."},{"area":"Webhook event types","evolution_path":"New domain events automatically become available for webhook subscription. Dispatcher subscribes to new event topics without core service changes."},{"area":"Gateway implementation technology","evolution_path":"Start with a proven gateway (Kong, Envoy, AWS API Gateway). Policy engine is a sidecar/plugin. Can swap gateway tech without rewriting policy logic."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Identity header contract (X-Authenticated-Identity, X-Consumer-Type)","reason":"All backend services depend on these headers for identity. Changing the header contract requires coordinated updates across all services. Lock this contract early and version it."},{"area":"Rate limit key structure in Redis","reason":"Changing the key schema (e.g., adding a new dimension) requires migration of all active counters. Design the key schema carefully upfront."},{"area":"Network isolation between external and internal traffic","reason":"This is a security invariant, not a flexibility point. Internal services must never be reachable from external networks. This must be enforced at infrastructure level (VPC, network policies) and never relaxed."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes a single point of failure","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy gateway replicas behind a load balancer with health checks. Use multiple availability zones. Implement circuit breakers so a single backend failure doesn't cascade through the gateway. Load test to establish capacity limits."},{"risk":"Redis rate limit store outage disables rate limiting","severity":"high","mitigation":"Deploy Redis in HA mode (Sentinel or Cluster). Implement fail-open with aggressive alerting. Consider local in-memory fallback rate limiting (approximate but better than nothing) during Redis outages."},{"risk":"Strangler fig migration leaves some services partially migrated for too long","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Set a hard deadline for migration completion. Track migration progress per service. Lint CI pipelines to detect auth code in services that should have been migrated."},{"risk":"Partner webhook delivery reliability","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Implement exponential backoff with configurable max retries. Dead letter queue for permanently failed deliveries. Partner dashboard showing delivery status. HMAC signatures so partners can verify authenticity."},{"risk":"Gateway header injection spoofing — external caller sends X-Authenticated-Identity header directly","severity":"high","mitigation":"Gateway MUST strip all internal headers (X-Authenticated-Identity, X-Consumer-Type) from incoming requests before processing. This is non-negotiable. Add integration tests that verify stripping."},{"risk":"Inconsistent rate limit behavior during gateway rolling deployments","severity":"low","mitigation":"Redis-backed rate limiting is deployment-independent since state is external. New gateway replicas pick up counters immediately."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (weeks 1-3): Deploy a single edge gateway (recommend Kong or Envoy-based) with the mobile auth plugin (JWT validation) and Redis rate limiting. Migrate 1-2 high-traffic mobile-facing services through the gateway using strangler fig. Strip auth from those services. Phase 2 (weeks 4-6): Add partner ingress with API key auth and higher rate limits. Implement webhook dispatcher as a standalone service consuming from the message bus. Migrate partner-facing services. Phase 3 (weeks 7-8): Deploy internal gateway (or activate service mesh like Istio/Linkerd). Enforce network policies isolating internal from external traffic. Phase 4 (ongoing): Migrate remaining services, remove legacy auth code, harden with penetration testing.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/gateway/mobile-ingress.yaml — Kong/Envoy config for mobile consumers","infra/gateway/partner-ingress.yaml — Kong/Envoy config for partner consumers","infra/gateway/internal-gateway.yaml — service mesh or internal routing config","infra/gateway/policies/rate-limits.yaml — rate limit tier definitions (mobile: 100/min, partner: 1000/min)","infra/gateway/policies/auth.yaml — auth strategy config per ingress","infra/redis/rate-limit-cluster.yaml — Redis HA deployment for rate limiting","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — new service: event consumer, delivery engine, retry logic","libs/policy-engine/ — shared auth + rate limit decision library (or gateway plugin)","infra/network-policies/ — Kubernetes NetworkPolicy or VPC rules isolating internal traffic","tests/integration/gateway/ — header stripping tests, rate limit accuracy tests, auth flow tests"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use 12-factor principles for gateway config (environment-driven, no hardcoded secrets). Rate limit keys follow the pattern {consumer_type}:{identity_id}:{window_start}. All gateway plugins must be idempotent and stateless. Webhook payloads use CloudEvents spec for interoperability. API versioning via URL path (/v1/) not headers for partner simplicity.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it handles only cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing, observability)","Do NOT share a single Redis instance for rate limiting and application caching — isolate to prevent cache eviction from breaking rate limits","Do NOT allow backend services to be reachable without going through a gateway — enforce with network policies, not convention","Do NOT implement rate limiting per-instance without shared state — this gives N × limit effective capacity with N replicas","Do NOT forget to strip internal headers from incoming external requests — this is the most common gateway security vulnerability","Do NOT migrate all services to the gateway simultaneously — use strangler fig to derisk incrementally"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-ingress gateway topology is well-established pattern for multi-consumer platforms","Redis-backed distributed rate limiting is industry standard","Strangler fig migration is the safest path from current state","Header stripping and network isolation are non-negotiable security requirements"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-native) depends on team expertise and existing infra which are unknown","Redis HA topology depends on cloud provider and existing Redis usage","Whether the team has Kubernetes or another orchestration platform affects network policy implementation"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on Kubernetes or similar container orchestration","The team has operational experience with Redis","Backend services communicate via HTTP/gRPC (not legacy protocols)","A message bus (Kafka, RabbitMQ, etc.) exists or can be introduced for webhook event delivery"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress channels (mobile, partner, internal), a shared policy engine for auth and rate limiting, and a service mesh for internal traffic isolation. This eliminates auth inconsistency, centralizes cross-cutting concerns, and creates clear security boundaries between external and internal traffi
